title-group.article-title: aNy-way ICA and its application to estimate cortico-thalamo-cerebellar functional links in schizophrenia

article-meta.aff: 1Tri-Institutional Center for Translational Research in Neuroimaging and Data Science (TReNDS), Georgia State University, Georgia Institute of Technology, Emory University, Atlanta, GA, USA
2Marcus Autism Center, Children’s Healthcare of Atlanta, Emory University School of Medicine, Atlanta, GA, USA

4Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Georgia Institute of Technology, Atlanta, GA, USA

abstract.p: Multimodal data collected by international and national biobanking efforts have distinct scales and model orders and provide unique and complementary insights into disease mechanisms. We propose a novel, flexible and efficient data fusion approach—aNy-way independent component analysis (aNy-way ICA). aNy-way ICA fuses N-way multimodal or multidomain data by optimizing the entire loading correlation structure of linked components via Gaussian independent vector analysis (IVA-G) and simultaneously optimizing independence via separate ICAs. This allows for distinct model orders for different modalities/domains and multiple linked sources detection across any number of modalities or domains without requiring orthogonality constraints on sources. Simulation results demonstrate that aNy-way ICA identifies the designed sources and loadings, as well as the true covariance patterns, with improved accuracy compared to other approaches, especially under noisy conditions. Applying aNy-way ICA to fuse 4D multi-domain fMRI data in schizophrenia, we identified a cortico-thalamo-cerebellar circuit, highlighting the functional linkages among higher order thalamic nuclei, the visual cortex, default mode network, and the posterior lobe of cerebellum. Their function links were replicated in two independent datasets. The connection among higher order thalamic nuclei, the visual cortex, and default mode network discriminates schizophrenia from controls and this aberrant connection is related to multiple cognitive deficits in both discovery and replication datasets, indicating the identified cortico-thalamo-cerebellar circuit may underlie "cognitive dysmetria" in schizophrenia.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Data fusion is an important analytical approach to perform integration of multiple data types collected from the same individuals—such as imaging, genetics, and behavioral measures. The goal is to extract coherent and unique patterns or brain networks that may be missed when analyzing each data type or each brain network separately. By capturing shared and distinct patterns across modalities or brain networks, data fusion can enhance the sensitivity and specificity of biomarker discovery for complex brain disorders. Over the past decades, several data fusion approaches have been proposed to uncover latent components/sources that explain variation across datasets. These methods aim to identify either shared structures across modalities or both shared and modality-specific features. For instance, joint independent component analysis (jICA) (Calhoun et al., 2006; Moosmann, Eichele, Nordby, Hugdahl, & Calhoun, 2008), parallel multilink jICA (Khalilullah et al., 2023), and linked ICA (Groves, Beckmann, Smith, & Woolrich, 2011) have focused on identifying shared loadings across modalities. Multiset canonical correlation analysis (mCCA) maximizes correlation linkage across modalities directly but is limited to orthogonal features. Meanwhile, mCCA+jICA (Sui et al., 2011) proposed a mCCA preprocessing in order to improve the correspondence across modalities and more closely satisfy the assumption of shared identical loadings prior to jICA, enabling N-way multimodal fusion. While there is no theoretical limit to the number of modalities analyzed together by jICA, mCCA, or the two-step approach mCCA+jICA, these approaches are generally limited to extracting only shared features and require the same number of components from each modality.

Disjoint subspace analysis using ICA (DS-ICA) (Adali, Akhonda, & Calhoun, 2019) and parallel ICA(pICA) (Liu et al., 2009) were developed to capture both common and distinct structures from two modalities. DS-ICA applies jICA on the common subspace extracted with principal component analysis (PCA) and CCA (Levin-Schwartz, Song, Schreier, Calhoun, & Adali, 2016), and utilizes separate ICAs on the remaining (distinct) subspace. pICA proposes to integrate separate (parallel) ICAs, one per modality, with simultaneous maximization of correlation between specific multimodal loading pairs. Thus, both independence and linkage are optimized together rather than in two steps. However, pICA (and 3-way pICA) (Liu et al., 2009; Vergara et al., 2014) is limited in the number of modalities and linked components it can robustly detect since it optimizes individual correlation pairs rather than the entire underlying linkage structure.

We proposed a new model called aNy-way ICA that overcomes these limitations (Duan, Calhoun, Liu, & Silva, 2020). aNy-way ICA optimizes the entire loadings correlation structure of linked components via Gaussian independent vector analysis (IVA-G) (Anderson, Adali, & Li, 2012) and simultaneously optimizes independence via separate (parallel) ICAs. Therefore, aNy-way ICA can detect multiple linked sources over any number of modalities or domains without requiring orthogonality constraints on sources and permitting a different number of sources for different modalities/domains. Here, we expand it with comprehensive theoretical developments, detailed implementation and simulation procedures, and additional real human brain data analyses.

Parallel to the burgeoning interest in multimodal fusion, there is a growing focus on fusing multi brain network domain data, including regions such as the thalamus, cerebellum, and cortical areas (P. H. Chen, Ye, Jin, Zhu, & Wang, 2019; Collin et al., 2012; Ferri et al., 2018; Kim et al., 2020). Traditional data-driven functional connectivity approaches, e.g., group independent component analysis (GICA) (Calhoun, Adali, Pearlson, & Pekar, 2001), often decompose whole-brain fMRI data into a single model order (Calhoun & Adali, 2012). However, this may not be optimal as different brain domains have different spatial organizations (Bijsterbosch et al., 2018; Diedrichsen, 2006; Iglesias et al., 2018; Tzourio-Mazoyer et al., 2002) and subdivisions of specific domains (e.g., thalamus) have distinct connectivity with other domains (e.g., cortex and cerebellum) (Giraldo-Chica & Woodward, 2017; Gong et al., 2019; Ha et al., 2023; Yuan et al., 2016; M. Q. Zhang et al., 2021). Recent studies employing domain-wise brain decomposition have revealed crucial insights on brain functionalities and enhanced sensitivity in detecting subdomain networks potentially overlooked in whole-brain connectivity studies (Dobromyslin et al., 2012; Hwang, Bertolero, Liu, & D'Esposito, 2017). Meanwhile, functional parcellations of specific regions or domains, such as the thalamus (S. Zhang & Li, 2017) and cerebellum (Dobromyslin et al., 2012; Ren, Guo, & Guo, 2019), have revealed informative details at higher spatial scales and revealed distinct connectivity with other domains/networks (Giraldo-Chica & Woodward, 2017; Gong et al., 2019; Ha et al., 2023; Yuan et al., 2016).

Andreasen and colleagues suggests disrupted prefrontal-thalamic-cerebellar circuitry in schizophrenia (SZ) patients, leading to "cognitive dysmetria" characterized by difficulties in prioritizing, processing, coordinating, and responding to information (Andreasen, Paradiso, & O′Leary, 1998). The thalamus serves as a passive relay station for integrating sensory information and is crucial for information processing between cortical regions (Hwang et al., 2017). Impairments in thalamus structure (Pergola, Selvaggi, Trizio, Bertolino, & Blasi, 2015) and thalamocortical connectivity (Anticevic et al., 2014; Avram, Brandl, Bauml, & Sorg, 2018; P. H. Chen et al., 2019; Gong et al., 2019; Sheffield et al., 2020; Yao, Neggers, Kahn, & Thakkar, 2020) have been extensively documented in SZ patients. The cerebellum, renowned for its diverse roles in motor learning, working memory, emotion, and language (Ren et al., 2019), exhibits both anatomical and functional alterations in SZ (Andreasen & Pierson, 2008; Ding et al., 2019; X. Li, Liu, Yang, Zhang, & Lui, 2022). Aberrant thalamus-cerebellum connectivity (Ha et al., 2023) and altered cerebro-cerebellar connections (Collin et al., 2012; Kang, Chung, Lee, & Bang, 2024; Kim et al., 2020) have also been reported in SZ.

Studies to date have employed region of interest (ROI)-based approaches to explore finer-grained thalamocortical connectivity (P. H. Chen et al., 2019), cerebro-cerebellar connectivity (Collin et al., 2012; Kim et al., 2020), or cortico-thalamo-cerebellar connectivity in SZ (Ha et al., 2023). These approaches primarily rely on anatomical definitions of ROIs without leveraging functional and connectivity information, and may miss relevant subregions crucial for the disease. To our knowledge, no study has investigated the links within the cortico-thalamo-cerebellar circuit using data-driven fusion approaches with variable model orders for each domain in SZ.

We used aNy-way ICA (Duan et al., 2020) to conduct domain-specific granular parcellation and optimize connections between subdivisions of different domains. By utilizing aNy-way ICA, we can perform targeted cortico-thalamo-cerebellar connectivity analysis while simultaneously optimizing inter-domain connections and separating sources within each domain. Unlike the traditional approaches mentioned above, which assume an identical number of components across all domains, aNy-way ICA allows for distinct model orders for each domain. This added flexibility makes it better suited to capture the complex and heterogeneous neurobiology of SZ. To evaluate its effectiveness, we first validate aNy-way ICA using simulated sMRI, fMRI, and EEG data under a range of scenarios, and compare its performance to widely used multimodal fusion approaches such as MCCA and MCCA +jICA. To examine its performance on multidomain fusion of 4-D human fMRI data, we apply aNy-way ICA to integrate data from the thalamus, cerebellum, and cortical regions extracted from resting-state fMRI data collected from 311 subjects (151 with SZ) within the Function Biomedical Informatics Research Network (FBIRN) cohort (Keator et al., 2016). In this application, we employ distinct model orders for each domain to identify cortico-thalamo-cerebellar subdomain linkages and evaluate their clinical relevance. We further validate these identified linkages in two independent replication datasets: one comprising 364 subjects from the Maryland Psychiatric Research Center (MPRC) cohort (Adhikari et al., 2019) ), and the other comprising 148 individuals from the Center for Biomedical Research Excellence (COBRE) cohort (Aine et al., 2017). Our results demonstrate that the identified cortico-thalamo-cerebellar subdomain linkages are consistently and largely replicated across both the COBRE and MPRC datasets. Moreover, we observe that the identified thalamus-cortex connection distinguishes SZ patients from controls and is associated with various cognitive scores in both the discovery and replication datasets.

sec.title: Method and Materials
sec.p: In this section, we first introduce the aNy-way ICA algorithm and its simulation design. We then describe the application of aNy-way to 4-D human brain multidomain (i.e., cortico-thalamo-cerebellar) fusion in SZ and the subsequent statistical analyses.

sec.title: aNy-way ICA
sec.p: aNy-way ICA (Duan et al., 2020) aims to simultaneously maximize the independence of sources while minimizing the mutual information between subspace component vectors (SCV). SCV represents correspondence across modalities in subsets of sources. As shown in Fig.1, without loss of generality, we consider three modalities: sMRI, fMRI, and EEG, each with 5, 4, and 6 sources, respectively. While the total number of sources Cm is different per modality, at most K=4 SCVs (maximum number of one-to-one correspondences across all modalities) can be extracted. For each modality, ICA decomposes the data Xm (dimension: N×Lm; m=1,2,3 modality indices; N is the number of subjects; Lm is the feature dimensionality) into a source matrix Sm (dimension: Cm×Lm) with corresponding loading matrices Am (dimension: N×Cm), i.e., Xm=AmSm.

The source matrix is estimated by the product of a weight matrix Wm and data as Sm=WmXm. The loading matrix is then reconstructed as the product of data and pseudo-inverse of the source matrix Am=XmSm−, a key strategy of this work. SCVs are then formed by the concatenation of linked loadings across the three modalities, where the total number of SCVs K is the minimum component number among the three modalities. Assuming each SCV follows a multivariate Gaussian distribution, mutual information among SCVs is minimized utilizing IVA-G since that has been shown to correspond to mCCA’s generalized variance (GENVAR) approach without orthogonality constraints (Anderson et al., 2010).

The cost function of aNy-way ICA is thus defined as:
(1) maxw∑m=1MHym−λ⋅Ia1,⋯,aK
where M is the total number of modalities, Hym represents the entropy of ym (the same as infomax ICA cost (Bell & Sejnowski, 1995)), ym is a nonlinear transformation of the bias-adjusted source um (ym=11+e−um, um=sm+bm, bm is a bias), Ia1,⋯,aK is the mutual information among SCVs ak, λ is a regularizer to balance between independence (ICA) and mutual information (IVA-G) optimization. The entropy of ym (Bell & Sejnowski, 1995) and mutual information among SCVs ak (Anderson et al., 2012) are defined in Equations 2–3, respectively:
(2) Hym=−Elnpymym
(3) Ia1,⋯,aK=∑k=1KHak−Ha1,⋯,aK=∑k=1KHak−Hx1S1−P1⊤,⋯,xkSk−Pk⊤=∑k=1KHak−∑m=1MlogdetSm−Pm⊤−HX1,X2,⋯,Xm=∑k=1KHak−∑m=1MlogdetSm−Pm⊤−C1
where pymym is the probability density function of the vector ym. The k-th SCV ak can be formed as ak=APk⊤, where Pk⊤ is akin to a permutation matrix that selects the loadings from the k-th component of each modality, and A=A1,⋯,AM is a concatenation of loadings from all modalities. Ha1,⋯,aK=∑mMlogdetSm−Pm⊤−HX1,X2,⋯,Xm is the joint entropy of all SCVs, which simplifies to a determinant function on the sources after noting the entropy of the data is constant. ∑k=1KHak denotes the sum of the marginal entropy of all SCVs, and Hak represents the marginal entropy of the k-th SCV (i.e., the concatenation of loading vectors of the k-th component across M modalities). We assume each SCV forms a zero-mean (with covariance Σk) multivariate Gaussian distribution (IVA-G model) to leverage the second-order statistical information across modalities. Then Hak can be defined as:
(4) Hak=E−logpak≈1N∑n=1N−logpakn=1N∑n=1N−logdet2πΣk−12+12akn⊤Σk−1akn

Plugging Equation 4 into Equation 3, and combining it with Equation 2, we can get the final form for aNy-way ICA cost. We then solve aNy-way ICA by SGD, utilizing the relative/natural gradients (Pierre Comon, 2010) of Equations 2–3 with respect to W, which are given, respectively, as:
(5) ∇WmH=I+1−2ymum⊤Wm
(6) ∇WmI=−Wm−⊤KmV1m−S˙m−Wm−Wm−⊤Fm⊤Tm−⊤Qm⊤Wm
where I is an identity matrix, 1 is a column vector of ones, ⊤ is the matrix transpose, Km=diagstdV1m⊤ is a diagonal matrix, stdV1m⊤ is a vector with the standard deviations from each row of V1m⊤, and V1m⊤=Um⊤Xm is based on eigenvalue decomposition (EVD) of Σm=EVDUmΛmUm⊤, with Σm=xmxm⊤Lm−1 being the sample covariance matrices from each modality Xm (Xm has zero mean rows and columns). S˙m−=S˙m1−⋯S˙mK−Pm is an intermediate gradient for the pseudo-inverse of the source matrix of the m-th modality, and S˙mk−=−1N−1Xm⊤zmk with zmk=akak⊤ak−1P1m⊤. Note that P1m⊤ selects the m-th column from the matrix to its left, and Pm expands the concatenation of S˙mk− vectors from Lm×K to Vm×Cm (effectively inserting zero columns as needed). Finally, approximating the determinants in the SCV joint entropy term as the product of singular values of Sm−Pm⊤=SVDU2mDmV2m⊤ (via SVD) and collecting constant terms into Fm=U2m⊤V1m−⊤Km and Qm=Pm⊤V2m, yields the final form in Equation 6, where Tm=FmWm−1Qm.

Since the source/loading ordering from separate ICAs per modality would be arbitrary (not linked), it is important not to over-emphasize independence early during the optimization. We also observed improved loading linkage (SCV) and source alignment by initially optimizing only IVA-G for a few steps/epochs before optimizing aNy-way ICA. To provide an initial effective (moderately linked) starting point for aNy-way ICA optimization, we propose to initially optimize only IVA-G until IVA-G cost is stabilized (usually around 50 steps) and then use the weight matrix obtained from IVA-G to project/align the data, which is then employed as the input for aNy-way ICA. Moreover, the regularizer parameter λ is initialized with 1 and adaptively adjusted by annealing it according to the entropy trend during the optimization: whenever entropy decreases with a slope larger than a threshold (here, 10−4), this triggers λ to be reduced by a factor (here, 0.8).

sec.title: Simulation
sec.p: Simulated sMRI, fMRI, and EEG data were employed to validate the proposed aNy-way ICA, where sMRI and fMRI sources were simulated with the SimTB toolbox (Erhardt, Allen, Wei, Eichele, & Calhoun, 2012). For EEG sources, we used a subset of simulated independent Laplacian time series in (Silva, Plis, Adali, Pattichis, & Calhoun, 2021), which had an autocorrelation around 0.85 between timepoint t and t−1, and 0.2 between t and t−10. Each SCV was sampled from a zero mean m-dimensional multivariate Gaussian distribution with unique covariance structures, where the correlations granularly varied from 0.2 to 0.8 to mimic correlations observed in real human brain data. SCVs were then organized into loading matrices Am. If applicable, extra loadings for non-linked sources were sampled from a standard Normal distribution and padded to Am. Finally, the data was generated as the product of the loading and source matrices as Xm=AmSm. The following scenarios were then examined:

sec.title: Scenario 1:
sec.p: All 3 modalities had the same number of sources Cm=7. The number of subjects was fixed at N=500. Feature dimensions Lm for sMRI, fMRI and EEG data were 31064 (voxels), 17420 (voxels), 4444 (time points), respectively (same Lm was used for scenario 2). Comparisons were performed against both mCCA and mCCA+jICA.

sec.title: Scenario 2:
sec.p: sMRI, fMRI, and EEG data had a different number of sources. In part (a), we assessed the effect of varying the number of linked SCVs K=3,5,7,9,11 while keeping N=500 fixed. The number of sources Cm for sMRI, fMRI and EEG data were set as K+1, K, and K+2, respectively, except for K=11 where C1=C2=11, and C3=12. In part (b), the number of sources Cm for sMRI, fMRI and EEG data were fixed at 8, 7, 9, respectively, while the subject number N varied from 100 to 1000. For each subject number, 10 sets of SCV/loadings were generated and run for each modality. Finally, in part (c), we assessed the effect of additive Gaussian white noise with Cm fixed at 8, 7, 9 for sMRI, fMRI, and EEG, respectively, and N fixed at 500. SNR varied from 0db to 30dB with a step size of 5dB. For each SNR level, the noise samples Eim were generated 5 times, yielding noisy data XimNoisy=Xm+Eim, i=1,…,5 for modality m. Note: scenarios 1, 2(a), and 2(b) were noise-free.

The following component-wise performance measures were computed per modality for aNy-way ICA, mCCA, and mCCA+jICA: correlations between recovered sources and ground-truth, as well as correlations between recovered loadings and ground-truth. To examine SCV recovery, we also calculated the mean square error (MSE) value between the recovered and ground-truth SCV cross-correlations (linkages), considering the diagonal blocks and off-diagonal portions separately, as well as together (whole matrix). For mCCA and mCCA+jICA, we estimated components under both settings when K=minmCm and K=maxmCm for all modalities (two separate runs). For scenario 2(a), correlations were averaged across matched components per modality, while for scenarios 2(b) and 2(c), correlations were averaged across runs and matched components. MSE values were averaged across runs.

sec.title: Samples
sec.p: Resting state fMRI datasets from three independent multicenter studies, i.e., FBIRN (Keator et al., 2016), COBRE (Aine et al., 2017), and MPRC(Adhikari et al., 2019), were used to test the feasibility and robustness of aNy-way ICA in identifying cortico-thalamo-cerebellar functional links and whole-brain functional network connectivity (FNC) in individuals with SZ and healthy controls (HCs). Each study was approved by the institutional review board and all participants provided written informed consent.

The discovery sample was composed of 311 individuals (age: 37.88±11.26 years) recruited from the FBIRN project that also passed quality control (QC, see details later) (Keator et al., 2016), including 151 individuals with SZ (36 female) and 160 HCs (45 female). All participants are adults (18–62 years old), and their cognitive performance was measured by the Computerized Multiphasic Interactive Neurocognitive System (CMINDS) (van Erp et al., 2015). Diagnosis of SZ was determined using DSM-IV (American Psychiatric, 1994). Table I summarizes the demographic and cognitive scores of included FBIRN subjects. Age and sex were matched between individuals with SZ and HCs (Table 1).

Two replication datasets were used to validate the results. The first replication dataset was collected from 148 participants passed QC (65 SZ patients and 83 HCs; 33 females; age: 37.87±12.77 years) in the COBRE cohort (Aine et al., 2017). Their cognition was evaluated using the MATRICS Consensus Cognitive Battery (MCCB) (Nuechterlein et al., 2008). Diagnosis of SZ was confirmed by two research psychiatrists using the Structured Clinical Interview for DSM-IV Axis I Disorders (First & Gibbon, 2004). Demographics and cognitive scores of COBRE participants are listed in Table 2. Age and sex were matched between SZ patients and HCs (Table II).

Another replication dataset included 364 participants recruited from the MPRC cohort (Adhikari et al., 2019) that passed QC (135 SZ patients and 229 HCs; 192 females; age: 39.88±14.72 years). Diagnoses were confirmed using the Structured Clinical Interview for Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders-IV (DSM-IV)(Association, 1996). Table III lists the demographics of MPRC participants. Age was matched between SZ patients and HCs (Table III).

sec.title: fMRI data acquisition and preprocessing
sec.p: The acquisition information of each fMRI dataset can be found in the supplementary information. All fMRI data were preprocessed using statistical parametric mapping (SPM12, http://www.fil.ion.ucl.ac.uk/spm/) following the NeuroMark fully automated spatially constrained ICA pipeline (Du et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2021) implemented in the GIFT software (http://trendscenter.org/software/gift). Specifically, subject head motion was corrected using rigid body motion correction, followed by slice-timing correction to adjust timing difference in slice acquisition. The slice-timing corrected fMRI data were further normalized to the standard Montreal Neurological Institute (MNI) space using an echo planar imaging (EPI) template (Calhoun et al., 2017). Normalized fMRI data were resampled to 3 × 3 × 3 mm3 (for extraction of thalamus and cerebellum) and 6 × 6 × 6 mm3 (for extraction of regions excluding thalamus and cerebellum, see details later) isotropic voxels separately and further spatially smoothed using a Gaussian kernel with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 6 mm.

NeuroMark QC (J. Chen et al., 2022; Du et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2021) was applied to all preprocessed scans. We excluded (1) subjects with head motions of any frames greater than 3 mm translation or exceeding 3° rotation (compared to the first frame after discarding the first five frames to account for signal equilibrium and participants’ adaptation to the scanner’s noise); (2) subjects with number of time points less than 120; (3) subjects with poor normalization quality.

sec.title: fMRI feature extraction in the thalamus, cerebellum, and cortex
sec.p: Thalamus, cerebellum and cortex regions (i.e., all other regions excluding thalamus and cerebellum, we referred it as cortex since they are primarily cortical regions) were defined and extracted from preprocessed fMRI data using the automated anatomical labeling (AAL) atlas (Tzourio-Mazoyer et al., 2002). To make feature dimensions of subdomains (thalamus, cerebellum, cortex) compatible and speed up the convergence of domain-wise fMRI data fusion, thalamus and cerebellum were extracted from fMRI data with 3 × 3 × 3 mm3 resolution, yielding 609 voxels for thalamus and 7217 voxels for cerebellum. Cortical regions were extracted from fMRI data with 6 × 6 × 6 mm3 resolution, yielding 5916 voxels.

sec.title: 4-D multidomain fusion using aNy-way ICA
sec.p: For each subdomain (thalamus, cerebellum, cortex) fMRI data, time courses were concatenated along subject direction to form a two-dimensional feature matrix with dimensions N×LN=totalnumberofsubjects×numberoftimepoints;L=numberofvoxels. Subject-wise mean removal was then applied, and the resulting data were input into aNy-way for domain-wise data fusion. aNy-way ICA simultaneously maximizes the independence of components within each subdomain while maximizing the connectivity between these subdomains. The component number was estimated separately for each subdomain based on the order selection method proposed in (Y. O. Li, Adali, & Calhoun, 2007). Assuming the minimum component number across the thalamus, cerebellum, and cortex is K, aNy-way ICA can effectively optimize K cortico-thalamo-cerebellar functional linkages (i.e., triplets or SCVs).

After aNy-way ICA converged, the sources (representing spatial maps of intrinsic connectivity networks (ICNs)) and their corresponding loadings (representing time courses (TC)) were obtained for the thalamus, cerebellum, and cortex, separately. A series of postprocessing steps were conducted on the TCs to eliminate noise sources, including (1) removal of linear, quadratic, and cubic trends; (2) adjusting motion effect using a multiple regression model with six realignment parameters and their temporal derivatives as independent variables; (3) de-spiking to detect and exclude outliers; and (4) band-pass filtering with [0.01–0.15] Hz.

Beyond identifying linked subdomain SCVs, aNy-way ICA decompositions enable the computation of whole-brain FNC to further investigate group differences (SZ vs. HC). Both K cortico-thalamo-cerebellar functional linkages and whole-brain FNC were derived from the postprocessed TCs. Specifically, K cortico-thalamo-cerebellar triplets (i.e., SCVs) were formed by concatenating the postprocessed TCs of the first K components from the thalamus, cerebellum, and cortex subdomains. Cross-correlation of these K triplets was computed using Pearson correlation and then Fisher-transformed to quantify cortico-thalamo-cerebellar functional linkages. Whole-brain FNC was computed in a similar manner, with cross-correlation estimated using all components from the thalamus, cerebellum, and cortex subdomains.

Multiple comparison was corrected over whole-brain FNCs using a false discovery rate (FDR) of p<0.05 (‘mafdr’ command in Matlab 2023a) (Duan, Premi, et al., 2021; Storey & Tibshirani, 2003) to take dependencies of connections within SCVs into account. SCVs were considered significantly interlinked when all their pair-wise connection were whole-brain wide significant (i.e., passed FDR at p<0.05 correction over whole-brain FNCs).

sec.title: Statistical inference
sec.p:
For each significantly interlinked SCV, we computed pair-wise subdomain connection for each participant and examined their ability for discriminating SZ from controls using a N-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) model:
Apair-wisesubdomainconnection=β0+β1×diagnosis+β2×age+β3×gender+β4×meanframe-wisedisplacement+β5×site+ε.
where each pair-wise subdomain connection is treated as the response variable and diagnosis is the predictor. Age, sex, mean frame-wise displacement (FD) and site are covariates of no interest. FDR at p<0.05 was applied to correct for comparisons from all pair-wise subdomain connections tested. SZ vs. HC difference of the whole-brain FNC was also examined using the abovementioned N-way ANOVA model, and multiple comparison was corrected over all whole-brain FNCs at FDR p<0.05. We further tested the associations between pair-wise subdomain connections and cognitive variables listed in Tables I–II using a N-way ANOVA model:
Apair-wisesubdomainconnection=β0+β1×acognitionvariable+β2×age+β3×gender+β4×meanFD+β5×site+ε.

Similarly, each pair-wise subdomain connection is treated as the response variable and age, sex, mean FD and site are covariates of no interest. Cognitive variables are the predictors. FDR at p<0.05 was applied to correct for comparisons from all pair-wise subdomain connections tested.

sec.title: Replicability of subdomain connections
sec.p: We assessed the replicability of the identified subdomain linkages, their group differences (SZ vs. HC) and associations with cognition, as well as whole-brain FNC group differences in COBRE and MPRC datasets. For each replication dataset, we applied the same preprocessing steps as in the discovery (FBIRN) dataset and projected the decomposed subdomain ICNs from FBIRN onto COBRE and MPRC data to extract the corresponding TCs (Duan et al., 2023; Duan, Jiang, et al., 2021). The resulting TCs underwent the same post-processing steps, including detrending, motion correction, de-spiking, and band-pass filtering. Since cognitive performance data included in FBIRN were not available in the MPRC dataset but were available in comparable formats for the COBRE dataset, we specifically examined the replicability of associations between subdomain connections and cognition in the COBRE dataset.

Consistent with the FBIRN dataset, we used Pearson correlation tests to evaluate subdomain linkages and applied N-way ANOVA models to assess group difference (SZ vs. HC) and associations with cognition for each pair-wise subdomain connection in the COBRE and MPRC datasets. A pair-wise subdomain linkage was considered replicated if its p-value was less than 0.05 and it exhibited the same directionality as in the FBIRN dataset. The group difference (SZ vs. HC) for a pairwise subdomain connection was considered replicated if (1) the connection was replicated; (2) the p-value for SZ vs. HC difference was below 0.05; and (3) the directionality aligned with the FBIRN findings. A similar criterion was applied to assess the replicability of associations between pair-wise subdomain connections and cognition in the COBRE dataset.

sec.title: Results of Simulation
sec.p: The recovery accuracies of SCVs under scenario 1 (i.e., seven nonoverlapping sources for sMRI, fMRI, and EEG data (Duan et al., 2020)) are plotted in Fig. 2 (a)–(d), where aNy-way ICA recovered all designed SCV structures very well (the MSE value was 2.8×10−4). Both mCCA and mCCA+jICA failed to recover, as indicated by the large values on off-diagonal positions, likely due to overfitting of correlations and inability to estimate non-orthogonal sources in mCCA, plus potential residual source misalignments from mCCA leaking into jICA for mCCA+jICA and overly strong assumption on shared loadings in mCCA+jICA. Fig. 2 (e)–(f) demonstrates that aNy-way ICA accurately recovered both sources and loading coefficients for all three modalities, while the source and loading accuracies were low for most independent components (ICs) recovered by mCCA and mCCA+jICA.

Performance measures for scenario 2(a) are shown in Fig. 3. aNy-way ICA accurately recovered components and loadings for sMRI and fMRI data, with slightly reduced recovery accuracy for EEG data (especially for 9 and 11 SCVs). Lower accuracies for EEG data are likely due to its relatively small feature dimensionality L3=4444 and weaker correlation linkages with other modalities within each SCV (Fig. 2 (a)). Overall, aNy-way ICA outperformed mCCA+jICA and mCCA in terms of source and loading recovery. mCCA+jICA and mCCA decompositions with the minimum component number of the three modalities had higher accuracies compared to those obtained with the maximum component number. As the SCV number increased (e.g., 9 or 11 SCVs), source and loading accuracies from both mCCA and mCCA+jICA dropped. The log10-transformed MSE values of the covariance structure of SCVs recovered by aNy-way ICA for scenario 2(a) are plotted in Supplementary Fig. 1 (a). aNy-way ICA fully recovered the designed covariance structure when SCV number was 3, 5, and 7, and largely recovered it when SCV number was 9 and 11 (sMRI and fMRI were aligned well but not sMRI-EEG and fMRI-EEG when SCV number was 9 and 11).

Fig. 4 demonstrates performance measures for scenario 2(b). As the number of subjects increased, the recovery accuracies of sources and loadings for fMRI and EEG, as well as the covariance structure of SCVs, increased, although there were some variations when the subject number was less than 400. Variation of accuracies of EEG data from aNy-way ICA was probably due to weak covariance structures generated from few samples. Log10-transformed MSE values for the whole matrix, diagonal blocks, and off-diagonal blocks of the recovered SCV cross-correlation are plotted in Supplementary Fig. 1 (b). As the number of subjects increased, the MSE values decreased (except for N=400 and N=1000).

Performance measures for scenario 2(c) are plotted in Fig. 5. Increasing the strength of noise superimposed on data, recovery accuracies of sources, and loadings dropped. aNy-way ICA outperformed mCCA and mCCA+jICA when SNR ≥ 5dB (when SNR = 5dB, the accuracy of EEG component and loading matrices recovered from aNy-way ICA was similar to that from MCCA and MCCA+jICA). When SNR = 0db, aNy-way ICA, mCCA, and mCCA+jICA failed to recover the sources and loadings. Overall, in contrast to using MCCA and MCCA+jICA to decompose all three modalities into the maximum component number of the three modalities, using MCCA and MCCA+jICA to decomposing data into the minimum component number achieved slightly higher component and loading accuracy. The log10-transformed MSE values of the whole matrix, diagonal blocks, and off-diagonal blocks of the recovered SCV cross-correlation under scenario 2(c) are plotted Supplementary Fig. 1 (c), where increased noise (smaller SNR) produces larger MSE values.

sec.title: Results of Human Brain Data
sec.p: We estimated 7 components for the thalamus, 11 components for the cerebellum, and 29 components for the cortex using the method in (Y. O. Li et al., 2007). Thus, the total number of SCV is seven (i.e., the minimum component number among the three subdomains). Among the identified seven subdomain SCVs, one SCV (Fig. 6, SCV 6) was significantly interlinked after FDR at p<0.05 correction. The thalamus component (blue color in Fig. 6 (a)) was positively and significantly connected with the cerebellum component (green color in Fig. 6 (a), r=0.16, p=6.19×10−3, Fig. 6 (b)). The cortex component (hot color in Fig. 6 (a)) was significantly anti-correlated with the thalamus (r=−0.15, p=1.01×10−2, Fig. 6 (c)) and the cerebellum component (r=−0.20, p=5.41×10−4, Fig. 6 (d)). Supplementary Fig. 2 plots the identified thalamus, cerebellum, and cortex components overlaid to their corresponding masks. The thalamus component highlighted medial and posterior thalamus (e.g., the mediodorsal and the pulvinar nuclei). The cerebellum component highlighted Crus I and declive, and the cortex component highlighted cuneus, precuneus, lingual, and posterior cingulate cortex.

The connection between thalamus and cortex in SCV 6 showed significant difference between SZ and HC (Fig. 7(a), p=3.77×10−7, Cohen’s d (referred as d hereafter) = 0.62), where SZ patients had weaker anti-correlation (i.e., hyperconnectivity) compared to HCs. Stronger thalamus-cortex connection in SCV 6 was significantly corrected with lower CMINDS composite score (Fig. 7(b), r=−0.25, p=1.87×10−4), slower processing speed (Fig. 7(c), r=−0.18, p=7.02×10−3), and poorer reasoning/problem solving skills (Fig. 7(d), r=−0.17, p=8.09×10−3), poorer verbal learning ability (Fig. 7(e), r=−0.17, p=7.48×10−3), poorer attention/vigilance (Fig. 7(f), r=−0.22, p=1.15×10−3) and poorer working memory performance (Fig. 7(g), r=−0.18, p=6.73×10−3). Stronger thalamus-cortex connection in SCV 6 was marginally related to poorer visual learning abilities (Supplementary Fig. 3(a), r=−0.12, p=0.04).

In the whole-brain FNC analysis, 254 out of 1081 total connection pairs were significantly linked after FDR at p<0.05 correction (see their connectogram in Supplementary Fig. 4). Moreover, 47 out of the 254 significantly linked pairs showed significant SZ vs. HC difference after FDR at p<0.05 correction. Fig. 8(a) plots the connectogram of 47 FNCs showing significant SZ vs. HC difference and the connectogram of corresponding SZ vs. HC difference (Cohen’s d value) is plotted in Fig. 8 (b).

sec.title: Replication in the COBRE dataset
sec.p: The interconnections of the identified SCV 6 were replicated in the COBRE dataset (Fig. 9 (a)). The thalamus component had a significant and positive association with the cerebellum component (r=0.19, p=6.95×10−3, the left panel in Fig. 9 (a)). The cortex component showed significant anticorrelations with the thalamus (r=−0.22, p=3.03×10−3, the middle panel in Fig. 9 (a)) and the cerebellum components (r=−0.21, p=3.03×10−3, the right panel in Fig. 9 (a)).

In the COBRE dataset, the thalamus-cortex connection in SCV 6 was significantly and negatively associated with MATRICS composite score (Fig. 9 (b), r=−0.20, p=2.02×10−2), processing speed (Fig. 9 (b), r=−0.32, p=1.13×10−4), reasoning/problem solving skills (Fig. 9 (b), r=−0.18, p=4.19×10−2), and visual learning abilities (Supplementary Fig. 3(b), r=−0.21, p=0.01). The thalamus-cortex connection in SCV 6 did not show significant difference between SZ and HCs p=0.09. In the whole-brain FNC analysis, 6 out of 47 FNCs showing significant SZ vs HC difference in the FBIRN (discovery) dataset were replicated in the COBRE dataset, and the connectogram of their Cohen’s d values for SZ vs. HC differences is plotted in Supplementary Fig. 5.

sec.title: Replication in the MPRC dataset
sec.p: Two out of the three interconnections of the identified SCV 6 were replicated in the MPRC dataset. The cortex component was significantly anti-correlated with the thalamus (r=−0.11, p=0.03, Fig. 10 (a)) and the cerebellum components (r=−0.18, p=6.71×10−4, Fig. 10 (b)). SZ patients also had significantly weaker anti-correlation between thalamus and cortex TCs in SCV 6 compared to HCs (d = 0.29, p=9.70×10−3, Fig. 10 (c)). In the whole-brain FNC analysis, 11 out of 47 FNCs showing significant SZ vs HC difference in the FBIRN (discovery) dataset were replicated in the MPRC dataset, and the connectogram of their Cohen’s d values for SZ vs. HC differences is plotted in Fig. 10 (d).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: In this paper, we used a novel approach called aNy-way ICA to enable flexible fusion of N-way multimodal or N-way 4-D multidomain fMRI data that allows for a different number of sources per modality/domain. The proposed aNy-way ICA simultaneously optimizes the independence of sources within modalities/domains and the correlations among their expression levels across modalities/domains without imposing orthogonality constraints. When applied to the fusion of thalamus, cerebellum and cortical regions extracted from 4-D fMRI data, aNy-way ICA allows to simultaneously identify multiple cortico-thalamo-cerebellar functional linkages and enables granular source separation within each domain by allowing distinct spatial scales and overlapping sources (rather than nonoverlapping subregions in ROI-based approaches) per domain (i.e., thalamus/cerebellum/cortex in this case). Furthermore, aNy-way facilitates the computation of more refined whole-brain FNC from the fusion results of 4-D multidomain data, thereby enabling a detailed examination of functional connectivity perturbations in individuals affected by specific brain disorders.

Simulation results demonstrated that aNy-way ICA reliably recovered sources and loadings, as well as the true covariance patterns, with improved accuracy compared to mCCA and mCCA+jICA under various scenarios, including (1) all three modalities have the same number of sources (e.g., 7 sources, an ideal case for mCCA and mCCA+jICA models); (2) different modalities have different sources and with varying number of SCVs, sample size, or level of noise superimposed on the data. The improved accuracy is particularly appreciated under noisy conditions.

Applying aNy-way ICA to fuse thalamus, cerebellum and cortical regions extracted from 4-D fMRI data collected from HCs and individuals with SZ, we identified one cortico-thalamo-cerebellar functional linkage (SCV 6) in the discovery dataset (FBIRN) and largely replicated this cortico-thalamo-cerebellar circuit in two independent replication datasets (COBRE and MPRC). This cortico-thalamo-cerebellar circuit highlights the functional connections among medial and posterior thalamus, Crus I, declive and cortical regions in the visual network (e.g., cuneus and lingual) and default mode network (DMN, e.g., precuneus and posterior cingulate cortex). The identified anti-correlation between medial and posterior thalamus and cortex (mostly in the visual network and DMN) in SCV 6 was significantly weaker in SZ compared to HCs in both FBIRN and MPRC datasets. More importantly, stronger connectivity between medial/posterior thalamus and DMN/visual network was reliably related to more severe cognitive deficits in both FBIRN and COBRE datasets, including lower CMINDs/MATRICS composite score, slower processing speed, impaired visual learning abilities and reduced reasoning/problem-solving skills.

Our identified thalamus component highlights higher order thalamic nuclei (e.g., the mediodorsal and the pulvinar nuclei), displaying similar spatial patterns as those reported involving in visual process (Steiner et al., 2020; Tu et al., 2015; Yuan et al., 2016; D. Y. Zhang et al., 2008). The mediodorsal and pulvinar have shown to be largely connected to DMN (Ha et al., 2023; Steiner et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2016), and their connectivity was related to processing speed, selective attention and cognitive flexibility (Steiner et al., 2020). Moreover, the connections between the visual cortex and the pulvinar nucleus have been well documented in humans (Cortes, Ladret, Abbas-Farishta, & Casanova, 2024; C. Li, Chen, Han, Chui, & Wu, 2012; Sabatinelli et al., 2011; Steiner et al., 2020; Yuan et al., 2016; D. Y. Zhang et al., 2008; Zuo et al., 2010), monkeys (Adams, Hof, Gattass, Webster, & Ungerleider, 2000; Baldwin, Kaskan, Zhang, Chino, & Kaas, 2012), prosimian primates (Purushothaman, Marion, Li, & Casagrande, 2012), as well as mice (Scholl, Foik, & Lyon, 2021; Takahashi, 1985; Tohmi, Meguro, Tsukano, Hishida, & Shibuki, 2014). Furthermore, the identified thalamus-cerebellar connection was supported by a prior finding that the posterior thalamus was connected to inferior cerebellum (Crus I) in individuals with early-onset schizophrenia (M. Q. Zhang et al., 2021). Importantly, the identified reduced anti-correlation (i.e., hyperconnectivity) between higher order thalamic nuclei and the visual network in SZ aligned well with the findings that SZ patients presented decreased negative correlation between visual cognition network and the dorsomedial part of thalamus (Gong et al., 2019), as well as the entire thalamus (Ferri et al., 2018), compared to HC. Aberrant FC between the left thalamus and the visual cortex has been documented to be related to attention deficits in schizophrenia (Yamamoto et al., 2018).

The approach and findings presented in this study should be considered in context with its strengths and limitations. The proposed aNy-way flexibly integrates multimodal or 4-D multidomain fMRI data with allowing distinct spatial scales per modality/domain, facilitating finer-grained exploration of brain circuits underlying complex brain disorders. One potential limitation of aNy-way is that aNy-way ICA optimizes multimodal/multidomain linkage within each minibatch defined in infomax (Bell & Sejnowski, 1995) using IVA-G for efficient alignment of sources, which may be not scalable to large datasets due to the inherent time complexity of the IVA-G model (Anderson et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2023; Vu et al., 2024). However, this limitation can be potentially relieved by employing efficient variants of IVA models (Gabrielson, Sun, Akhonda, Calhoun, & Adali, 2023; Sun et al., 2023; Vu et al., 2024), which will be tested in our future studies. Given this study’s focus on the development and initial validation of the aNy-way ICA method in simulated and schizophrenia fMRI data, future work is needed to explore possible moderating effects of schizophrenia clinical variables and treatments.

In conclusion, we proposed a flexible N-way multimodal/multidomain data fusion approach called aNy-way ICA to account for variable spatial scales across different modalities/domains, enabling comprehensive and finer-grained exploration of coherent multimodal or multidomain patterns. We validated the effectiveness of aNy-way ICA using both simulated data and human brain fMRI data. Using the proposed aNy-way ICA, we identified and replicated a cortico-thalamo-cerebellar functional circuit, highlighting the functional linkages among higher order thalamic nuclei, the visual cortex, and the posterior lobe of the cerebellum. The identified connection between higher order thalamic nuclei and visual cortex differentiates individuals with SZ from HC and aberrant connection between these regions is associated with multiple cognitive deficits observed in individuals with SZ. This underscores the clinical relevance and potential utility of the identified cortico-thalamo-cerebellar connectivity patterns in elucidating the pathophysiology of SZ and its associated cognitive deficits.

ack.p: We greatly appreciate individuals who participated in this research study. We would also like to thank research coordinators and principal investigators involved in data collection and data sharing. This work is supported in part by NSF grants 2112455 and 2316421 to VDC. This work was also supported by the National Institutes of Health grants: 1U24 RR021992 (Function Biomedical Informatics Research Network) and 1U24 RR025736-01 (Biomedical Informatics Research Network Coordinating Center), and 1R01MH130595 to JL.

sec.title: Data and Code Availability
sec.p: The code for aNy-way ICA is publicly available in the Fusion ICA Toolbox (FIT, https://trendscenter.org/software/FIT/). COBRE data can be downloaded from COINS (https://coins.trendscenter.org/), MPRC data can be access from the NIMH Data archive. FBIRN data are not available due to IRB restrictions.

caption.p: Diagram of aNy-way ICA. Sources Sm are estimated by maximization of independence in each modality separately. Corresponding loadings Am are then organized into SCVs ak, followed by minimization of their mutual information with IVA-G, which amounts to maximization of the correlation structure within the SCV without orthogonality constraint (Anderson, Li, & Adali, 2010). The model is optimized with stochastic gradient descent (SGD) until convergence.

Scenario 1, cross-correlation of SCVs (the order of modalities is sMRI, fMRI and EEG for each SCV) from (a) ground-truth, (b) aNy-way ICA, (c) mCCA+jICA, and (d) mCCA. Correlation coefficients between (e) recovered sources and ground-truth, and between (f) recovered loadings and ground-truth for each IC: sMRI (downward triangle), fMRI (star), and EEG data (upward triangle) from aNy-way ICA (red), mCCA+jICA (green) and mCCA (blue).

Scenario 2(a), where the number of the SCVs varied from 3 to 11. Recovered source accuracies for (a) sMRI, (b) fMRI, and (c) EEG data, and loading accuracies for (d) sMRI, (e) fMRI, and (f) EEG data using aNy-way ICA (red), mCCA+jICA with maximum (solid green) or minimum component number (dashed green), mCCA with maximum (solid blue) or minimum component number (dashed blue). Values are averaged across matched components per modality.

Scenario 2(b), where the number of subjects N varied from 100 to 1000. Recovered source accuracies for (a) sMRI, (b) fMRI, and (c) EEG data, and loading accuracies for (d) sMRI, (e) fMRI, and (f) EEG data. Values are averaged across runs and matched components per modality.

Scenario 2(c), where SNR varied from 30db to 0db. Recovered source accuracies for (a) sMRI, (c) fMRI, and (e) EEG data, and loading accuracies for (b) sMRI, (d) fMRI, and (f) EEG data. Values are averaged across runs and matched components per modality.

The identified cortico-thalamo-cerebellar SCV and their linkages in the FBIRN (discovery) dataset. (a) The Z-scored spatial map of the identified cortico-thalamo-cerebellar SCV and (b-d) corresponding pairwise connections: (b) thalamus-cerebellum, (c) thalamus-cortex, (d) cerebellum-cortex.

(a) Group difference and (b-g) associations with cognitive variables ((b) CMINDs composite, (c) processing speed, (d) reasoning/problem solving, (e) verbal learning, (f) attention/vigilance, (g) working memory) of the thalamus-cortex connection in SCV6 in the discovery dataset after adjusting effects from age, sex, site, and head motion. Red dots denote individuals with SZ and blue dots represent HCs.

(a) The connectogram of significant FNCs (the color of the edge represents correlation values between subdomain components) and (b) the connectogram of FNCs showing significant SZ vs HC difference (the color of the edge represents Cohen’s d values of SZ vs. HC difference).

(a) The pair-wise connections of the identified SCV 6 and (b) the association between thalamus-cortex connection in SCV 6 and cognitive variables in the COBRE dataset after adjusting effects from age, sex, site and head motion. Red dots denote individuals with SZ and blue dots represent HCs.

(a-b) The pair-wise connections of the identified SCV 6 and (c) group difference of the identified thalamus-cortex connection in SCV6 in the MPRC dataset after adjusting effects from age, sex, site and head motion. Red dots denote individuals with SZ and blue dots represent HCs. (d) The connectogram of FNCs replicated for SZ vs. HC difference in the MPRC dataset (the color of the edge represents Cohen’s d values of SZ vs. HC difference).
Demographic Information and Cognitive Scores for SZ and HC Individuals in the FBIRN Cohort.
Demographic Information and Cognitive Scores for SZ and HC Individuals in the COBRE Cohort.
Demographic Information of SZ and HC Individuals in the MPRC Cohort.
